docs.title: Hi, I'm Himani
docs.text:
I'm a technical writer with 8+ years of experience turning complex technical concepts into clear, accessible documentation. I specialize in API documentation, developer guides, and user manuals for SaaS platforms and developer tools.

My work bridges the gap between engineering teams and the people who use their products. I believe that great documentation is not just about explaining features—it's about empowering users to succeed.

docs.title: Professional Bio
docs.text:
I'm a technical writer based in India, specializing in developer documentation, API references, and user guides. Over the past 8+ years, I've worked with startups, enterprise SaaS companies, and open-source communities to create documentation that people actually want to read.

My background in computer science gives me the technical depth to understand complex systems, while my passion for clear communication ensures that understanding flows outward to users of all skill levels.

Whether you need API documentation, user guides, developer tutorials, or a complete documentation strategy, I'd love to hear about your project.

Response time: I typically respond within 24 hours on business days.

docs.title: What to Include in Your Message
docs.text:
To help me understand your needs, please include:

  • A brief description of your project or product
  • The type of documentation you need
  • Your timeline and budget (if applicable)

Looking forward to hearing from you.

docs.title: Building a Documentation Strategy from Scratch
docs.text:
April 15, 2026 :tags: documentation strategy, docs-as-code

A few months ago, I joined a startup that had zero documentation. The engineering team was drowning in support tickets, the sales team was creating their own slide decks to explain the product, and customers were frustrated.

Here's the framework I used to build a documentation strategy from nothing.

docs.title: Step 1: Map the User Journey
docs.text:
Before writing a single word, I mapped out every touchpoint a user has with the product:

  1. Discovery: Landing page, product overview, feature comparison
  2. Onboarding: Sign-up, quickstart, first successful action
  3. Daily Use: Feature guides, troubleshooting, best practices
  4. Advanced Use: API reference, integrations, customization
  5. Support: FAQs, community forums, contact channels

Each touchpoint is a documentation need.

docs.title: Step 2: Prioritize by Impact
docs.text:
You can't write everything at once. I used a simple framework:

  • High impact, low effort: Write these first (quickstarts, FAQs)
  • High impact, high effort: Plan and resource these (API docs, tutorials)
  • Low impact, low effort: Fill in during quiet periods
  • Low impact, high effort: Question whether they're needed at all

docs.title: Step 3: Choose Your Tools
docs.text:
I chose a docs-as-code approach:

  • Markdown for writing (familiar to engineers)
  • Git for version control and collaboration
  • MkDocs + Material for the static site generator
  • GitHub Actions for automated builds and deployment

This meant engineers could contribute to documentation through the same workflow they used for code.

docs.title: Step 4: Create a Style Guide
docs.text:
A concise style guide ensures consistency:

  • Tone: conversational but professional
  • Voice: active, direct, and helpful
  • Code examples: always include expected output
  • Terminology: maintain a glossary of product-specific terms

docs.title: Step 5: Set Up Feedback Loops
docs.text:
Documentation without feedback is guesswork. I set up:

  • A "Was this helpful?" widget on every page
  • Monthly review sessions with support engineers
  • Quarterly developer surveys
  • Analytics to track which pages are most (and least) visited

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Within 4 months:

  • Support tickets dropped 40%
  • 12 engineers were contributing to documentation
  • Customer satisfaction increased from 3.2 to 4.5
  • Documentation became a selling point in enterprise deals

docs.title: The Key Takeaway
docs.text:
You don't need a perfect plan to start building documentation. You need a framework, the right tools, and a commitment to iteration. Start small, measure everything, and improve continuously.

Have questions about building a documentation strategy? Let's chat. Find me on LinkedIn.

docs.title: Writing for Developers: Lessons from 8 Years in the Trenches
docs.text:
May 1, 2026 :tags: writing craft, developer experience

When I started my career as a technical writer, I thought my job was to explain things clearly. I was wrong. My job is to help developers accomplish their goals.

Here's what eight years of writing for developers has taught me.

docs.title: 1. Start with the User's Goal, Not Your Product's Features
docs.text:
Nobody opens your documentation because they want to read about your product. They open it because they have a problem to solve. Frame every piece of documentation around what the user is trying to achieve.

Bad: "The CloudSync API supports GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE operations."

Good: "To create a new resource, send a POST request to /api/v1/resources."

docs.title: 2. Code Examples Are Your Most Important Content
docs.text:
Developers learn by doing. A well-crafted code example is worth a thousand words of explanation. Make sure every example:

  • Runs without modification (copy-paste ready)
  • Shows real-world scenarios, not abstract "foo/bar" examples
  • Includes expected output so readers can verify success

docs.title: 3. Write the Quickstart First
docs.text: Before you write the full reference, write a quickstart that gets a new user from zero to working in under 5 minutes. If you can't do that, your product is too complex—or your documentation isn't.

docs.title: 4. Error Messages Are Documentation Too
docs.text:
When something goes wrong, your error messages are the documentation your users see. Make them helpful:

  • Explain what went wrong in plain language
  • Suggest a specific next step
  • Link to relevant documentation

docs.title: 5. Documentation Is a Product
docs.text:
Treat your documentation like a product, not a byproduct. It needs:

  • A clear purpose and audience
  • Regular maintenance and updates
  • Feedback loops with users
  • Metrics to measure success

docs.title: The Bottom Line
docs.text:
Great documentation doesn't just explain features—it empowers developers to succeed. When you shift your perspective from "explaining" to "enabling," everything changes.

What's the best piece of documentation you've ever used? I'd love to hear about it. Reach out on Twitter or LinkedIn.

docs.title: CloudSync REST API Reference
docs.text: Client: CloudSync Technologies Duration: 6 months (ongoing maintenance) Role: Lead Technical Writer

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: CloudSync needed a comprehensive API reference for their cloud infrastructure platform. The API had grown to 50+ endpoints across multiple services, and the existing documentation was scattered across Confluence pages, Slack threads, and developer knowledge.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
1. Inventory & Audit: Cataloged all existing endpoints, identified gaps, and prioritized by usage analytics.
  2. OpenAPI Specification: Created and maintained OpenAPI 3.0 specs as source of truth, integrated into the CI/CD pipeline.
  3. Interactive Examples: Provided code samples in Python, JavaScript (Node.js), and cURL for every endpoint.
  4. Developer Feedback Loop: Ran usability sessions with 10 developers, iterated based on their feedback.
  5. Automated Testing: Integrated documentation tests into the CI pipeline to catch breaking changes before they reached production.

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
• Complete API reference with 50+ endpoints
  • OpenAPI 3.0 specification (YAML)
  • Interactive code examples in 3 languages
  • Authentication guide (OAuth 2.0, API keys)
  • Error reference with troubleshooting steps
  • Rate limiting and best practices guide

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
• 40% reduction in API-related support tickets
  • 2.5x increase in developer portal engagement
  • 95% positive feedback in developer survey

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text: OpenAPI, Swagger UI, Redoc, Python, JavaScript, Postman, GitHub Actions, Markdown

docs.title: Documentation Strategy for DevOps Startup
docs.text: Client: DevTools Labs (Series A startup, 40 employees) Duration: 4 months Role: Documentation Consultant

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The startup had a great product but virtually no documentation. Engineering was overwhelmed with support questions, and the sales team struggled to explain the product's capabilities. They needed a documentation strategy from scratch.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
1. Content Audit & Architecture: Mapped out all documentation needs (user guides, API docs, tutorials, FAQs) and created an information architecture.
  2. Docs-as-Code Workflow: Set up documentation in Git alongside code, using Markdown and a static site generator. Engineers could contribute through pull requests.
  3. Style Guide: Created a concise style guide covering tone, terminology, code examples, and formatting standards.
  4. CI/CD Integration: Automated documentation builds and deployment through GitHub Actions. Every merge updated the live docs site.
  5. Training: Ran workshops with engineers on writing documentation, reviewing docs PRs, and using the new workflow.

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
• Information architecture document
  • Style guide (20 pages)
  • Documentation contribution guide for engineers
  • CI/CD pipeline for automated builds
  • Initial content: quickstart, 3 tutorials, API reference skeleton
  • Training materials and workshop recordings

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
• 50% reduction in engineering time spent on documentation tasks
  • 12 engineers contributing to documentation within 3 months
  • Customer satisfaction score increased from 3.2 to 4.5
  • Documentation became a selling point in enterprise deals

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text: MkDocs, Material for MkDocs, GitHub Actions, Markdown, Git, Vale (linting)

docs.title: Enterprise SSO Migration Guide
docs.text: Client: SecureAuth Inc. Duration: 3 months Role: Technical Writer

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: SecureAuth was transitioning enterprise customers from legacy username/password authentication to SAML-based single sign-on. The migration process was complex, and customers needed clear, actionable guidance to avoid downtime and security issues.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
1. Shadowed Support Engineers: Spent a week observing support calls to understand common pain points and failure modes.
  2. Task-Based Structure: Organized the guide around user tasks rather than product features—what the reader needs to do, not what the product can do.
  3. Visual Decision Trees: Created flowcharts to help administrators choose the right migration path based on their current setup.
  4. Rollback Procedures: Included comprehensive rollback steps for each migration phase.
  5. Peer Review: Had the guide reviewed by 3 support engineers and 2 customers before publication.

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
• 45-page migration guide (PDF + web)
  • Quick-reference checklist (1-page)
  • Troubleshooting FAQ (20+ entries)
  • Video walkthrough script (15 minutes)
  • Email notification templates for end-users

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
• 30% reduction in migration-related support tickets
  • Zero escalations during the first month of rollout
  • Guide adopted as template for future product migrations

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text: MadCap Flare, Confluence, Lucidchart, Google Docs, Markdown
